_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology and outbreak history from WHO Yellow Fever fact sheet 2024 and EYE strategy 2017-2026 progress reports. Virology and clinical course per Monath TP & Vasconcelos PFC. J Clin Virol 2015 (PMID 25510535), Barrett AD & Higgs S. Annu Rev Entomol 2007 (PMID 16846387), and Gardner CL & Ryman KD. Clin Lab Med 2010 (PMID 20513546). Vaccine safety per Lindsey NP et al. JAMA 2010 (PMID 20736473) and Staples JE et al. MMWR 2010 / WHO position paper 2013. PALM-style outbreak data per Angola 2016 and Brazil 2017-2019 sylvatic outbreaks. International Health Regulations Annex 7 vaccination certificate policy current to 2024.

h1Title: Yellow Fever: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Yellow fever is an acute viral hemorrhagic fever caused by a flavivirus transmitted by Aedes and Haemagogus mosquitoes in tropical Africa and South America. WHO estimates 84,000-170,000 severe cases and 29,000-60,000 deaths each year despite the availability of a highly effective single-dose live-attenuated vaccine licensed since 1937. After an incubation period of 3-6 days, about half of infected people remain asymptomatic; the other half develop a brief febrile illness with fever, headache, and myalgia. Approximately 15% of symptomatic patients enter a second toxic phase 24-48 hours after apparent remission, with jaundice, bleeding, renal and hepatic failure, and 30-60% mortality. Treatment is supportive only — no specific antiviral exists — so prevention through vaccination and vector control is the cornerstone of management. The WHO EYE (Eliminate Yellow fever Epidemics) strategy launched in 2017 aims to protect 1 billion people in 27 high-risk countries by 2026 through mass preventive vaccination campaigns. Most travellers visiting risk areas in Africa or the Americas need a single dose of 17D vaccine and a valid International Certificate of Vaccination or Prophylaxis (ICVP) for cross-border travel.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 84,000-170,000 severe cases and 29,000-60,000 deaths per year (WHO 2024)
keyStats.demographics: 47 countries endemic in Africa, 13 in Central and South America; over 90% of cases reported from Africa
keyStats.avgAge: Working-age adults dominate documented case counts; vaccine coverage in children under 5 averages 50-70% in endemic countries
keyStats.globalCases: Case-fatality rate 30-60% in those with severe disease; over 1 billion people targeted by WHO EYE strategy by 2026

definition: Yellow fever (ICD-10: A95) is an acute viral hemorrhagic fever caused by yellow fever virus (YFV), a single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus in the Flaviviridae family that is closely related to dengue, West Nile, and Zika viruses. The virus is transmitted to humans through bites of infected female mosquitoes in three distinct transmission cycles: jungle (sylvatic) yellow fever between forest mosquitoes (Haemagogus and Sabethes in the Americas; Aedes africanus in Africa) and non-human primates, with humans occasionally infected when entering the forest; intermediate (savannah) yellow fever in moist savannah regions of Africa where multiple Aedes species transmit virus between humans and monkeys in small villages; and urban yellow fever, in which Aedes aegypti transmits virus among humans in densely populated cities and produces large explosive outbreaks. The first phase of disease (period of infection) follows a 3-6 day incubation and consists of fever, headache, myalgia, backache, nausea, and conjunctival injection over 3-4 days. Roughly 85% of symptomatic patients recover at this point. The remaining 15% enter the period of intoxication after a brief remission of hours to 24 hours: high fever returns, jaundice appears, vomiting becomes intractable (often with hematemesis — the historical 'black vomit'), the kidneys fail, and bleeding becomes prominent. Up to 50% of patients who reach this phase die within 7-10 days. The disease is managed by infectious disease specialists, intensive care services, hepatology, and nephrology, with public-health response coordinated by national health ministries, WHO, GAVI, and UNICEF.

typesClassification.description: Roughly 50% of infections produce no symptoms; recognized only by serology in outbreak investigations.

typesClassification.type: Mild yellow fever (period of infection only)
typesClassification.description: Approximately 30-40% of symptomatic patients have a single 3-4 day febrile illness with headache, myalgia, conjunctival injection, and bradycardia despite fever (Faget's sign), then recover.

Approximately 15% of symptomatic patients enter the toxic phase after a brief remission: jaundice, hemorrhage, vomiting (sometimes hematemesis — 'black vomit'), renal failure, hepatic encephalopathy. Mortality 30-60%.
Severe end of the spectrum with disseminated intravascular coagulation, gastrointestinal bleeding, epistaxis, gum bleeding, and ecchymoses; classical historical phenotype.

Transmission cycle between forest mosquitoes and non-human primates with occasional human spillover. Dominates South American transmission and accounts for sporadic and outbreak cases in Bolivia, Brazil, Peru, Colombia, and Venezuela.

Aedes aegypti-mediated person-to-person transmission in dense human populations; produces explosive epidemics. Most recently re-established in Luanda, Angola in 2015-2016 with imported cases reaching China for the first time.

primarySymptoms: Sudden onset of fever above 39 °C with rigors, severe headache (often retro-orbital and frontal), and back pain 3-6 days after a mosquito bite in an endemic region.
Severe muscle pain particularly in the back and legs, malaise, and prostration during the first 3-4 days (period of infection).
Conjunctival injection without exudate, facial flushing, and a tongue with red edges and white furred center — historical clinical descriptions.
Relative bradycardia despite high fever (Faget's sign), an early clue that distinguishes yellow fever from many other tropical febrile illnesses.
Apparent improvement on day 3-4 with defervescence and reduced symptoms — the brief remission.
Return of fever, vomiting (sometimes with frank hematemesis — historic 'vomito negro' or 'black vomit'), and jaundice 24-48 hours after remission, marking the period of intoxication.
Worsening jaundice and right-upper-quadrant abdominal pain from acute hepatic injury.
Reduced urine output, dark or smoky urine, and rising creatinine from acute kidney injury.
Mucocutaneous bleeding — gum bleeding, epistaxis, conjunctival hemorrhage, petechiae, hematuria, melena — driven by hepatic synthetic failure and disseminated intravascular coagulation.
Confusion, agitation, and progressive obtundation from hepatic encephalopathy in the toxic phase.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden fever and headache 3-6 days after travel to or residence in an endemic region with no documented yellow fever vaccination
Bradycardia in a febrile traveller from sub-Saharan Africa or tropical South America (Faget's sign)
Conjunctival injection with facial flushing during a tropical febrile illness
Mild elevations in transaminases, raised bilirubin, or new proteinuria in the first 4 days of febrile illness
Recurrence of fever after apparent improvement — a red flag for impending toxic phase
emergencySigns: Hematemesis, melena, or massive gastrointestinal bleeding — toxic-phase hemorrhage requiring transfusion
Encephalopathy, seizures, or coma — hepatic failure with cerebral edema
Oliguria, anuria, or refractory hyperkalemia — acute kidney injury requiring dialysis
Profound hypotension with cold extremities — hemorrhagic or septic shock
Severe hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, or rapidly rising INR in the toxic phase

causes.cause: Yellow fever virus (YFV) transmitted by Aedes aegypti in the urban cycle
causes.description: Aedes aegypti is the principal urban vector and feeds on humans during daytime. Urban transmission cycles support explosive outbreaks in densely populated cities and account for the historical global spread of yellow fever via shipping during the 18th-19th centuries.

causes.cause: Yellow fever virus transmitted by Haemagogus and Sabethes mosquitoes in the South American sylvatic cycle
causes.description: Forest canopy mosquitoes (Haemagogus janthinomys, Sabethes chloropterus) transmit YFV between non-human primates and humans entering forest areas for work, hunting, or recreation. Drives sporadic cases and outbreaks in Brazil, Bolivia, Peru, Venezuela, Colombia, and adjacent countries.

causes.cause: Yellow fever virus transmitted by Aedes africanus and other Aedes species in the African sylvatic and intermediate cycles
causes.description: Aedes africanus maintains the forest cycle between monkeys and humans in tree canopies. Aedes furcifer-taylori, Aedes luteocephalus, and other Aedes species drive intermediate transmission in moist savannah regions of Africa where humans live in villages near forest edges.

causes.cause: Insufficient vaccination coverage in endemic populations and travellers
causes.description: Yellow fever vaccine has been licensed since 1937 and provides lifelong immunity in over 95% of recipients. Outbreaks recur where vaccine coverage falls below 60-80%, including during stock-outs, vaccine hesitancy, conflict, and rapid urbanization. Unvaccinated travellers to endemic regions remain a documented source of imported cases.

Deforestation, urban expansion into forest, climate-change-driven extension of Aedes vector range, and rapid urbanization create new conditions for both sylvatic and urban transmission. The 2017-2019 Brazilian sylvatic outbreak followed monkey die-offs in regions long considered low-risk.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or travel in endemic regions
riskFactors.description: 47 countries in tropical Africa (notably Nigeria, Democratic Republic of Congo, Ethiopia, Sudan, Angola) and 13 in Central and South America (Brazil, Bolivia, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela) are endemic. Risk extends across rural and increasingly some urban areas.

Unvaccinated residents and travellers face the highest risk. Vaccine provides lifelong protection in over 95%. Travel medicine clinics routinely vaccinate travellers to risk areas at least 10 days before travel.
Forest workers, hunters, soldiers, miners, ecotourists, and researchers entering jungle areas face elevated sylvatic exposure to canopy mosquitoes.
Densely populated cities with high Aedes aegypti density (Luanda, Kinshasa, parts of Brazilian cities) are at risk of urban epidemics if virus is reintroduced.

Yellow fever vaccine carries a slightly elevated risk of viscerotropic disease (YEL-AVD) and neurotropic disease (YEL-AND) in adults over 60 (about 1 per 100,000-400,000), infants under 9 months, and immunocompromised individuals. These groups need careful pre-vaccination evaluation.

riskFactors.factor: Severe immunosuppression (HIV with CD4 under 200, organ transplantation, certain biologics)
riskFactors.description: Live vaccines including YFV are contraindicated in severe immunosuppression because of risk of disseminated vaccine virus. Risk-benefit assessment required before vaccinating mildly immunocompromised travellers.

affectedDemographics: Tropical Africa accounts for over 90% of global yellow fever burden (WHO 2024), with the heaviest reported case counts from Nigeria, Democratic Republic of Congo, Ethiopia, Sudan, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and Senegal.

South America (Brazil, Bolivia, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Guyana, French Guiana, Suriname, Ecuador, Trinidad and Tobago) reports several hundred to several thousand cases per year depending on outbreak activity.
Asia and Australia have never reported autochthonous yellow fever despite the presence of Aedes aegypti — a long-standing public-health concern but unexplained absence.
Outbreaks have triggered emergency vaccine campaigns in Angola (2015-2016), Democratic Republic of Congo (2016), Brazil (2017-2019), Nigeria (2017-2020), and Ghana (2021-2022).
Imported cases occur in travellers returning to non-endemic countries; very rare deaths in unvaccinated travellers to Brazil have raised attention since 2017.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines compatible clinical illness, exposure history (recent travel to or residence in a yellow fever transmission zone), and laboratory confirmation. Routine bloods in the toxic phase typically show leukopenia followed by neutrophilia, very high transaminases (AST often greater than ALT and rising into the thousands), conjugated and unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, prolonged prothrombin time, thrombocytopenia, and rising creatinine. The specific diagnostic standard is detection of viral RNA by RT-PCR within the first 7-10 days of illness on blood or, in fatal cases, post-mortem liver tissue. Yellow fever IgM antibody is detectable from day 5-7 of illness and persists for months to years; cross-reactivity with other flaviviruses (dengue, Zika, Japanese encephalitis, West Nile) is significant and requires plaque-reduction neutralization testing (PRNT) at reference laboratories for confirmation. Vaccinated travellers retain IgG for life, so serology alone cannot diagnose acute infection in vaccinated patients without paired sera demonstrating a rise. Detection of yellow fever viral antigen (NS1) and immunohistochemistry of liver tissue (showing characteristic mid-zonal hepatocyte necrosis with Councilman bodies — eosinophilic apoptotic hepatocyte remnants) is a hallmark in post-mortem confirmation. Differential diagnosis is broad and includes other viral hemorrhagic fevers (Ebola, Lassa, Marburg, dengue, Crimean-Congo), severe malaria, leptospirosis, viral hepatitis (especially fulminant hepatitis A and E), severe sepsis, and toxic exposures. All suspected cases require notification to public-health authorities.

diagnosticTests.test: RT-PCR for yellow fever virus RNA
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms acute infection during the first 7-10 days of illness
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood or post-mortem tissue sample sent to a reference laboratory; result within 1-3 days. Most useful early in illness when viraemia is highest.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects recent infection from day 5-7 of illness
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood sample sent to a reference laboratory; result within 3-7 days. Cross-reaction with other flaviviruses requires confirmation by plaque-reduction neutralization testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms yellow fever-specific antibody and excludes cross-reaction with other flaviviruses
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood sample tested at a specialized reference laboratory; result within 2-4 weeks. Required for definitive serological confirmation in cross-endemic regions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades severity of hepatic injury and identifies impending hepatic failure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw with daily monitoring during admission. AST often rises into the thousands and exceeds ALT — characteristic of yellow fever.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects acute kidney injury, electrolyte derangement, and metabolic acidosis in the toxic phase
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood and urine testing daily during admission.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates pathognomonic mid-zonal hepatocyte necrosis and Councilman bodies in fatal cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed at autopsy or rarely on living patients; histology reviewed by an experienced pathologist.

treatmentOverview: There is no specific antiviral therapy for yellow fever; treatment is entirely supportive. Patients with confirmed or strongly suspected yellow fever should be admitted to an isolation room with mosquito-proof screening for at least 5 days of fever to prevent local transmission via Aedes aegypti. Initial care in mild disease focuses on rest, oral hydration, antipyretics (paracetamol — strictly avoiding aspirin and other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs because of bleeding risk), and observation for signs of toxic-phase deterioration. Patients entering the toxic phase require intensive-care admission with: aggressive intravenous fluid and electrolyte management, vasopressors for shock, mechanical ventilation if respiratory failure develops, renal replacement therapy for acute kidney injury, blood products (packed red cells, fresh frozen plasma, platelets, cryoprecipitate) for hemorrhage and coagulopathy, glucose support to counter hepatic hypoglycemia, lactulose and consideration of N-acetylcysteine for hepatic encephalopathy, and management of cerebral edema. Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics may be considered for suspected superimposed bacterial sepsis. Several investigational antivirals (sofosbuvir, favipiravir, TY014 monoclonal antibody) have been evaluated in case reports and early-phase trials with limited efficacy; none is routinely recommended. The single most effective intervention is prevention through vaccination. The live-attenuated 17D yellow fever vaccine is given as a single subcutaneous dose at 0.5 mL, with seroconversion in over 95% by day 10 and lifelong protection per 2013 WHO and SAGE position. Vaccine adverse events include mild fever and headache in 25%, anaphylaxis (1 per 130,000-250,000), vaccine-associated viscerotropic disease (1 per 100,000-400,000, mostly in adults over 60), and neurotropic disease (1 per 100,000-500,000). Contraindications include severe egg allergy, severe immunosuppression, infants under 6 months, and thymic disorders. Pregnancy is a relative contraindication — vaccinate only if travel cannot be deferred to a high-risk area. Outbreak control combines mass preventive vaccination campaigns, vector control of urban Aedes aegypti (source reduction, larviciding, indoor residual spraying), surveillance with serological investigation of suspected cases, and rapid deployment of vaccine stockpile via the International Coordinating Group on Vaccine Provision (ICG).

medicalTreatments.name: Aggressive intravenous fluid and electrolyte resuscitation
medicalTreatments.description: Crystalloid resuscitation guided by hemodynamic status, urine output, and lactate. Avoid over-resuscitation, which worsens cerebral oedema in hepatic failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces shock-related mortality; standard of care in toxic-phase yellow fever

medicalTreatments.name: Renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis or continuous renal replacement)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for acute kidney injury with oliguria, fluid overload, hyperkalemia, or uraemia. Continuous renal replacement therapy preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality reduction in patients with severe AKI; outcome depends on hepatic recovery

medicalTreatments.name: Blood product transfusion (packed cells, fresh frozen plasma, platelets, cryoprecipitate)
medicalTreatments.description: Used to treat coagulopathy and hemorrhage. Targets vary by clinical situation; massive transfusion protocols may apply in severe gastrointestinal hemorrhage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hemorrhagic mortality when delivered with adequate hepatic and renal support

medicalTreatments.name: Hepatic supportive care (glucose, vitamin K, lactulose, N-acetylcysteine)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous glucose to prevent hepatic hypoglycemia, vitamin K to address coagulopathy, lactulose for hepatic encephalopathy, and N-acetylcysteine for hepatic protection in fulminant hepatic failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supportive role; reduces complications and may improve survival in fulminant hepatic failure

medicalTreatments.name: Antipyretics (paracetamol only) and avoidance of NSAIDs
medicalTreatments.description: Paracetamol controls fever and discomfort. Aspirin and NSAIDs are strictly avoided because of bleeding risk in the toxic phase and worsening of acute kidney injury.

medicalTreatments.name: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics for superimposed bacterial sepsis
medicalTreatments.description: Used when bacterial sepsis cannot be excluded — common during prolonged ICU care. Ceftriaxone or similar covers common pathogens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality from secondary bacterial infection in critically ill patients

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation in fulminant yellow fever hepatitis
surgicalOptions.description: Considered in highly selected patients with isolated fulminant hepatic failure and otherwise good prognosis; only feasible in specialized transplant centres. Outcomes are limited by extra-hepatic involvement and viral persistence in the graft.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Case-series survival approximately 40-70% in carefully selected patients; very limited published experience

alternativeTreatments.name: Investigational antivirals (sofosbuvir, favipiravir, TY014 monoclonal antibody)
alternativeTreatments.description: Compassionate-use case reports and early-phase trials. No approved antiviral for yellow fever as of 2026; treatment remains supportive.

whySeeSpecialist: Suspected yellow fever requires urgent evaluation by infectious disease and intensive care services. Severe disease (toxic phase) demands ICU-level care with hepatology and nephrology support. Travel medicine clinics deliver pre-travel vaccination and certification.
doctorSelectionGuide: Pre-travel vaccination requires a designated yellow fever vaccination centre that can issue the International Certificate of Vaccination or Prophylaxis (yellow card). For suspected disease, seek care at a national reference laboratory or tertiary infectious disease unit.
hospitalCriteria: Critical-care capacity with renal replacement therapy
Blood bank capable of supporting massive transfusion
Access to RT-PCR and yellow fever IgM testing
keyFacilities: Tertiary infectious disease and intensive care units
Liver transplant centres for fulminant hepatic failure
Public-health emergency operations centres during outbreaks
preventionStrategies: Receive a single dose of 17D yellow fever vaccine at least 10 days before travel to or residence in an endemic country.
Carry the International Certificate of Vaccination or Prophylaxis (yellow card) for cross-border travel; many countries require it for entry from yellow-fever-endemic regions.
Use DEET-, picaridin-, or IR3535-based insect repellent during daytime in endemic regions; Aedes aegypti bites during the day.
Sleep under insecticide-treated bed nets and stay in screened or air-conditioned accommodation where Aedes density is high.
Eliminate standing water around dwellings (tyres, flower pots, water containers) where Aedes mosquitoes breed.
Participate in mass preventive vaccination campaigns where offered.
Maintain ongoing surveillance for monkey die-offs and febrile illness in endemic regions and report suspected cases promptly.
lifestyleModifications: Follow medical advice and travel-clinic instructions about vaccination, boosters, and certification.
Stay in screened or air-conditioned accommodation during travel to high-risk areas.
Wear long-sleeved shirts and long trousers in endemic regions, particularly during daytime.
Avoid the toxic-phase pitfalls — no aspirin or NSAIDs during a febrile tropical illness.
Report any febrile illness within 14 days of travel from an endemic region to a clinician promptly.
Continue regular medical follow-up if you have had a documented yellow fever vaccine adverse event; future live-vaccine decisions will need expert input.

recommended: Oral rehydration solution or balanced electrolyte drinks during fever
Bland, easily digestible foods (rice, toast, bananas) during nausea
Adequate protein intake during convalescence to support hepatic recovery
Iron- and folate-rich foods for survivors of severe disease with anemia
avoid: Alcohol during acute illness and recovery — direct hepatic injury
Raw or undercooked food and unsafe water during travel to endemic regions
High-fat fried foods during nausea phase

exerciseGuidelines: Bed rest during acute febrile illness. Gradual return to physical activity during convalescence over 2-6 weeks; severe disease with hepatic involvement may take 8-12 weeks for full recovery. Avoid contact sports until coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia resolve.
dailyManagement: Monitor temperature and report relapse after apparent improvement immediately.
Drink at least 2-3 L of fluids per day to support kidney recovery.
Avoid alcohol and unnecessary hepatotoxic medications during recovery.
Use mosquito repellent and bed nets in endemic regions during convalescence and re-exposure prevention.
Attend follow-up liver, kidney, and ophthalmology assessment as advised.
Document yellow fever vaccination and infection history for future travel medicine consultations.
prognosis: Outcome is determined primarily by whether the patient enters the toxic phase and by availability of intensive supportive care. Approximately 50% of infections are asymptomatic; among symptomatic patients, 85% recover after the period of infection alone. The 15% who progress to the toxic phase face 30-60% mortality — substantially worse in resource-limited settings without intensive care, blood-product support, and renal replacement therapy. Survivors of severe disease usually recover hepatic and renal function over weeks to months, although some retain residual liver-function abnormalities. There is no chronic carriage and no long-term viral persistence outside immune-privileged sites. Yellow fever vaccine produces lifelong protection in over 95% of recipients per WHO 2013 SAGE position, although a small minority of vaccinated travellers may benefit from boosters under specific circumstances (HIV infection, vaccination during pregnancy, post-hematopoietic stem-cell transplant). The 2017 WHO EYE strategy aims to vaccinate 1 billion people in 27 high-risk countries by 2026 through mass preventive campaigns, routine childhood immunization, and outbreak response.
recoveryTimeline: Mild yellow fever (period of infection only): full recovery within 1-2 weeks. Severe yellow fever survivors: hospital stay typically 2-3 weeks; full convalescence over 1-3 months. Liver function normalizes over 4-12 weeks; renal function over 4-8 weeks. Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance can persist for several months after severe disease.
complications: Fulminant hepatic failure with hepatic encephalopathy and cerebral oedema.
Acute kidney injury requiring renal replacement therapy.
Disseminated intravascular coagulation with severe hemorrhage.
Septic shock and secondary bacterial infections during prolonged ICU stay.
Yellow-fever-vaccine-associated viscerotropic disease (YEL-AVD) — rare (1 per 100,000-400,000) but with high mortality, especially in adults over 60.
Yellow-fever-vaccine-associated neurotropic disease (YEL-AND) — rare immune-mediated CNS reaction usually with good recovery.
Anaphylactic reactions to the egg-based vaccine — rare and managed by expert vaccination centres.

supportResources.name: WHO Yellow Fever
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, EYE strategy progress, vaccination policy, and outbreak situation reports.

supportResources.name: CDC Yellow Book — Yellow Fever Chapter
supportResources.description: Authoritative US traveller-focused guidance, vaccination indications, and country-specific recommendations.

supportResources.name: International Coordinating Group on Vaccine Provision (ICG)
supportResources.description: Manages global emergency stockpile of yellow fever vaccine for outbreak response.

supportResources.name: Pan American Health Organization — Yellow Fever
supportResources.description: Regional surveillance, outbreak alerts, and vaccination strategy for the Americas.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both are Aedes-borne flaviviral fevers in tropical regions. Dengue causes severe retro-orbital pain, biphasic rash, plasma leakage, and is diagnosed by NS1 antigen or PCR. Yellow fever produces marked hepatic injury (high transaminases, jaundice) and the characteristic toxic-phase return of fever, distinct from dengue's typical course.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can produce jaundice and renal injury in tropical regions. Leptospirosis features intense calf myalgia, conjunctival suffusion, lower transaminase rise (<5× upper limit), and responds to doxycycline. Yellow fever shows extreme transaminase elevation, marked hemorrhage, and characteristic exposure history with positive RT-PCR or IgM.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Severe falciparum malaria can produce jaundice, renal failure, and cerebral involvement. Malaria is diagnosed by blood film or rapid antigen test and treated with artemisinin therapy. Yellow fever lacks parasitemia and has characteristic transaminase pattern and IgM/PCR serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ebola Virus Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both are viral hemorrhagic fevers with hemorrhage and shock. Ebola has distinct epidemiology (African outbreaks, person-to-person spread via body fluids), profuse gastrointestinal symptoms, and confirmed by RT-PCR. Yellow fever is mosquito-borne, has a biphasic clinical course with hepatic predominance, and is vaccine-preventable.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis B
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute hepatitis B can produce fulminant hepatic failure but typically with more gradual onset over weeks, characteristic serology (HBsAg positive, IgM anti-HBc), and lack of acute mosquito exposure history. Yellow fever has sudden onset, biphasic course, characteristic toxic phase, and confirmation by yellow fever virus testing.

relatedConditions.name: Dengue Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Closely related flavivirus also transmitted by Aedes aegypti; major differential and cross-reactive in serology.

relatedConditions.name: Zika Virus
relatedConditions.relevance: Another Aedes-transmitted flavivirus with overlapping geographic distribution; serological cross-reactivity affects diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Japanese encephalitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Flavivirus sharing antigenic features; vaccine cross-reaction affects yellow fever serology.

relatedConditions.name: Ebola Virus Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Major viral hemorrhagic fever differential in sub-Saharan Africa.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Tropical fever with jaundice and renal injury; common differential in returning travellers.

faqs.question: What is yellow fever?
faqs.answer: Yellow fever is an acute viral hemorrhagic disease caused by a flavivirus transmitted by Aedes and Haemagogus mosquitoes in tropical Africa and South America. Most infections are mild or asymptomatic, but about 15% of symptomatic cases develop a toxic phase with jaundice, kidney failure, and bleeding, with 30-60% mortality.

faqs.question: How is yellow fever spread?
faqs.answer: Yellow fever virus is transmitted to humans by bites of infected female mosquitoes. Aedes aegypti drives urban epidemics, while Haemagogus and Sabethes mosquitoes drive sylvatic (jungle) transmission between primates and humans in South America. The disease is not spread directly person-to-person.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of yellow fever?
faqs.answer: Symptoms begin 3-6 days after a bite with sudden fever, headache, muscle and back pain, nausea, and conjunctival injection. About 15% progress after a brief remission to the toxic phase with jaundice, severe vomiting (sometimes black vomit), bleeding, and acute kidney injury, with high mortality.

faqs.question: Is yellow fever curable?
faqs.answer: There is no specific antiviral therapy for yellow fever; treatment is entirely supportive. Most patients recover with rest, fluids, and observation. Severe disease requires intensive care with renal replacement therapy, blood product support, and hepatic supportive care. The most effective intervention is prevention through vaccination.

faqs.question: Where is yellow fever found?
faqs.answer: Yellow fever is endemic in 47 countries in tropical Africa and 13 in Central and South America. Over 90% of cases are reported from Africa, with Nigeria, Democratic Republic of Congo, Ethiopia, and Sudan accounting for the largest burdens. The disease is not found in Asia or Australia despite Aedes aegypti's presence.

faqs.question: Do I need a yellow fever vaccine for travel?
faqs.answer: Many countries require proof of yellow fever vaccination for entry of travellers arriving from endemic regions. WHO recommends vaccination for all travellers aged 9 months and older going to endemic countries. Vaccinate at least 10 days before travel. Check current country-specific requirements before your trip.

faqs.question: How long does yellow fever vaccine protect you?
faqs.answer: A single dose of 17D yellow fever vaccine produces protective antibody in over 95% of recipients within 10 days and provides lifelong protection per WHO 2013 position. Some special populations (HIV-positive, vaccinated during pregnancy, post-hematopoietic stem-cell transplant) may benefit from booster doses.

faqs.question: What are yellow fever vaccine side effects?
faqs.answer: Most recipients experience only mild side effects: low-grade fever, headache, or injection-site soreness for a few days. Rare serious events include anaphylaxis (1 per 130,000-250,000) and vaccine-associated viscerotropic disease (1 per 100,000-400,000, mostly in adults over 60). Vaccinate at designated centres with pre-vaccination assessment.

faqs.question: Who should not get the yellow fever vaccine?
faqs.answer: Contraindications include severe egg allergy, severe immunosuppression (advanced HIV, chemotherapy, transplantation), thymic disorders, and infants under 6 months. Pregnancy is a relative contraindication — vaccinate only if travel to high-risk areas cannot be deferred. Adults over 60 require additional pre-vaccination evaluation.

faqs.question: How long does yellow fever last?
faqs.answer: The period of infection lasts 3-4 days. About 85% of symptomatic patients recover at this stage. The 15% who enter the toxic phase have a critical 7-10 day course with high mortality; survivors typically need 2-6 weeks for full convalescence. There is no chronic infection.

faqs.question: What is the toxic phase of yellow fever?
faqs.answer: The toxic phase is a return of severe illness 24-48 hours after apparent recovery in roughly 15% of symptomatic patients. It features jaundice, severe vomiting (sometimes hematemesis), kidney failure, mucocutaneous bleeding, and hepatic encephalopathy. Mortality is 30-60% even with intensive care.

faqs.question: Is yellow fever the same as hepatitis?
faqs.answer: No, although yellow fever can cause acute viral hepatitis with jaundice and very high transaminases. Yellow fever is caused by a flavivirus transmitted by mosquitoes and is vaccine-preventable. Viral hepatitis (A, B, C, D, E) is caused by different unrelated viruses with different transmission routes and treatments.

faqs.question: Can yellow fever be treated with antibiotics?
faqs.answer: No. Yellow fever is caused by a virus, so antibiotics have no direct effect on the disease. Empirical antibiotics are sometimes used to treat secondary bacterial infections during severe yellow fever, but treatment of the underlying viral disease remains supportive.

faqs.question: How is yellow fever diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses RT-PCR on blood within the first 7-10 days of illness, yellow fever IgM serology from day 5-7 onwards, and confirmatory plaque-reduction neutralization testing for definitive diagnosis. Post-mortem liver histology shows characteristic mid-zonal necrosis with Councilman bodies.

faqs.question: Is yellow fever contagious?
faqs.answer: Yellow fever does not spread directly from person to person under normal circumstances. Transmission requires an infected mosquito vector. Rare laboratory-acquired cases and breast-milk transmission to infants have been documented. Standard infection-control measures are still applied to confirmed cases in hospitals.

faqs.question: Why is it called yellow fever?
faqs.answer: The name 'yellow fever' refers to the jaundice that develops in the toxic phase, giving the skin and eyes a yellow color. Spanish colonial chroniclers used the term 'fiebre amarilla' from the 17th century. In Brazil and parts of Latin America the disease was historically called 'vomito negro' (black vomit) for its hemorrhagic feature.

faqs.question: Can you get yellow fever twice?
faqs.answer: Recovery from yellow fever produces lifelong immunity, so reinfection is essentially unheard of. The vaccine likewise produces lifelong protection in over 95% of recipients per WHO 2013 position. There is no chronic carrier state.

faqs.question: Is yellow fever a major problem in 2026?
faqs.answer: Yellow fever remains a significant global health threat. WHO estimates 84,000-170,000 severe cases per year. Recent outbreaks in Angola (2015-2016), DRC (2016), Brazil (2017-2019), and Nigeria (2017-2020) have triggered emergency vaccination campaigns. The WHO EYE strategy aims to protect 1 billion people by 2026.

faqs.question: What should I do if I think I have yellow fever?
faqs.answer: Seek urgent medical care if you develop fever within 14 days of travel to an endemic region. Tell the clinician about your travel history immediately. Avoid aspirin and other NSAIDs. Do not delay care if you experience jaundice, vomiting, reduced urine output, or any bleeding — toxic-phase disease requires intensive care.

faqs.question: Can I travel to areas with yellow fever?
faqs.answer: Yes, with appropriate vaccination at least 10 days before travel. Carry the International Certificate of Vaccination or Prophylaxis (yellow card). Use insect repellent, sleep in screened or air-conditioned rooms, and avoid daytime mosquito bites. Consult a travel medicine clinic 4-6 weeks before departure.

faqs.question: Is yellow fever vaccine safe in HIV?
faqs.answer: Yellow fever vaccine can be given to HIV-positive individuals with CD4 counts above 200 cells/µL and well-controlled HIV. Vaccine response may be weaker than in HIV-negative people, so booster doses are sometimes recommended. Severe immunosuppression (CD4 below 200 or active AIDS-defining illness) is a contraindication.

metaTitle: Yellow Fever: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Yellow fever: mosquito-borne flavivirus disease with jaundice and hemorrhage. Symptoms, 17D vaccine, prevention and travel certification reviewed.
